items.foco: Elevated intraocular pressure and optic nerve damage
items.title: IOP and axonal damage
items.contentHtml:
Elevated intraocular pressure and optic nerve damage

  • Normal intraocular pressure (IOP) ranges between 10-21 mmHg; Values ​​>21 mmHg are considered ocular hypertension, but damage can occur with IOP <22 mmHg (normal tension glaucoma).
  • Axonal damage is secondary to direct mechanical compression and ischemia of the optic disc, with loss of retinal ganglion cells.
  • The most important modifiable risk factor is elevated IOP; Every 1 mmHg reduction decreases the risk of progression by ~10%.
  • Test trap: Do not confuse ocular hypertension (elevated IOP without damage) with open angle glaucoma (requires structural/functional damage).

items.title: Gonioscopy: open angle
items.contentHtml:
Open iridocorneal angle in gonioscopy

  • Gonioscopy is mandatory to classify glaucoma; In open angle glaucoma (OAG) all the structures of the angle (Schwalbe's line, trabecular meshwork, scleral spur, ciliary band) are observed without iridocorneal contact.
  • Shaffer classification: grade III-IV (open angle, 20-40°); grade 0-I indicates angle closure.
  • Trap: the presence of pigmentation of the trabecular meshwork suggests pigmentary glaucoma (DDx).
  • In primary GAA there are no peripheral anterior synechiae or angle closure.

items.foco: Gradual loss of peripheral visual field
items.title: Campimetry: peripheral defect
items.contentHtml:
Gradual loss of the peripheral visual field

  • Automated campimetry (Humphrey, Octopus) detects early defects such as Bjerrum arcuate scotoma (respects the horizontal midline) and nasal step.
  • The loss begins in the nasal periphery and progresses centrally; advanced glaucoma leaves only a temporal island (tubular vision).
  • Diagnostic criteria: a reproducible defect in 2 of 3 visual field tests consistent with damage to the optic nerve.
  • Trap: the Amsler maneuver is not useful for glaucoma (it is used in maculopathies).

items.foco: Progressive papillary excavation in the fundus
items.title: Fundus: excavation
items.contentHtml:
Progressive papillary excavation in the fundus

  • The normal cup/disc (C/D) ratio is ≤0.4; a C/D >0.5 or asymmetry >0.2 between both eyes is suspicious for glaucoma.
  • Signs of glaucomatous damage: vertical excavation (loss of neuroretina), focal notch (notch), splinter hemorrhage in the optic disc.
  • Optical coherence tomography (OCT)measures the thickness of the retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) and the ganglion cell complex; values ​​< 1st percentile are abnormal.
  • Trap: a large excavation without loss of visual field may be physiological (high myopia).

items.title: Age as a risk factor
items.contentHtml:
Main risk factor: advanced age

  • The prevalence of primary open angle glaucoma (POAG) increases exponentially with age: <1% in <40 years, >6% in >80 years.
  • Aging reduces the drainage capacity of the trabecular meshwork and increases the stiffness of the connective tissue of the nerve.
  • Examination trap: advanced age is the main non-modifiable risk factor, but elevated IOP is the main modifiable risk factor.
  • In patients >65 years, screening with tonometry and fundus examination is recommended every 1-2 years.

items.foco: Association with myopia and African descent
items.title: Myopia and African descent
items.contentHtml:
Association with myopia and Afro-descendant race

  • The Afro-descendant race presents a 3-4 times greater risk of POAG, earlier diagnosis (5-10 years earlier) and worse response to medical treatment.
  • High myopia (> -6 D) doubles the risk of POAG, possibly due to alterations in scleral biomechanics and greater susceptibility from the optic nerve to the IOP.
  • Trap: in myopes, physiological papillary excavation can simulate glaucoma; always correlate with visual field and OCT.
  • DDx: secondary glaucoma in myopia due to pigment dispersion syndrome (open angle with pigment in trabecular meshwork).

items.title: 1st line: prostaglandins
items.contentHtml:
First-line treatment: prostaglandin analogues

  • The prostaglandin analogues (latanoprost, travoprost, bimatoprost, tafluprost) are the first-line treatment in POAG due to their effectiveness and once-daily dosage.
  • They reduce IOP by 25-35% by increasing drainage uveoscleral; maximum effect at 8-12 hours.
  • Adverse effects: conjunctival hyperemia, darkening of the iris (heterochromia), hypertrichosis of the eyelashes, possible cystic macular edema in aphakic patients.
  • Trap: prostaglandin analogues are contraindicated in neovascular glaucoma (they can exacerbate the inflammation).

items.foco: Latanoprost as the most used drug
items.title: Latanoprost: the most used
items.contentHtml:
Latanoprost as the most commonly used drug

  • Latanoprost 0.005% (Xalatan®) is the most prescribed prostaglandin analog due to its effectiveness, tolerance and cost.
  • Dose: 1 drop in the affected eye every night (nighttime administration optimizes the morning IOP peak).
  • Reduces IOP by ~30% from the first dose; stable effect in 2 weeks.
  • Trap: if you miss a dose, do not double the next one; continue with the usual dose the next day.
  • Relative contraindication: severe asthma (can trigger bronchospasm due to prostaglandin analogues).

items.title: Uveoscleral drainage
items.contentHtml:
Mechanism: increased uveoscleral drainage

  • Aqueous humor is drained by two routes: trabecular (conventional, dependent on IOP) and uveoscleral (independent of IOP, represents ~10-20% of total drainage).
  • prostaglandin analogues increase drainage. uveoscleral by activating matrix metalloproteinases that remodel the collagen of the ciliary muscle and supraciliary spaces.
  • This mechanism is additive to beta blockers, which reduce the production of aqueous humor.
  • Trap: prostaglandin analogs do not affect trabecular drainage; That is why they are effective even if the trabecular meshwork is damaged.

items.foco: Topical beta blockers reduce aqueous humor production
items.title: Topical beta blockers
items.contentHtml:
Topical beta blockers reduce aqueous humor production

  • Timolol 0.25%-0.5% (non-selective β1 and β2 beta blocker) is the most used; dose: 1 drop every 12 hours.
  • Reduces IOP by 20-25% by inhibiting adenylate cyclase in the ciliary epithelium, decreasing the production of aqueous humor.
  • Systemic adverse effects: bradycardia, bronchospasm (contraindicated in asthma/COPD), fatigue, depression.
  • Selective alternative: betaxolol (β1 selective) with lower bronchoconstrictor risk, but lower hypotensive efficacy.
  • Trap: the effect of topical beta-blockers on IOP can be attenuated with chronic use (tachyphylaxis).

items.foco: Surgery: trabeculectomy if medical treatment fails
items.title: Trabeculectomy
items.contentHtml:
Surgery: trabeculectomy if medical treatment fails

  • Trabeculectomy is the surgical gold standard when IOP is not controlled with drugs (maximum tolerated medical therapy) or there is progression of damage.
  • It consists of creating a fistula from the anterior chamber to the subconjunctival space, forming a filtration bleb.
  • It is used mitomycin C intraoperatively to prevent blister healing (greater long-term success).
  • Complications: hypotony, endophthalmitis, suprachoroidal hemorrhage, choroidal detachment.
  • Trap: laser trabeculoplasty (ALT/SLT) is a prior alternative to trabeculectomy in patients with Mild-moderate POAG.

items.foco: Monitoring with Tonometry, Campimetry and OCT
items.title: Glaucoma monitoring
items.contentHtml:
Monitoring with Tonometry, Campimetry, and OCT

  • POAG monitoring includes tonometry (IOP measurement at each visit), automated campimetry (every 6-12 months to detect progression), and OCT (annual to evaluate RNFL and ganglion cell complex).
  • The target IOP in POAG is individualized: 20-30% reduction compared to baseline; in advanced glaucoma, goal <15 mmHg.
  • Progression: defined by worsening of field defects (event or trend) or loss of RNFL thickness on OCT.
  • Trap: isolated IOP is not sufficient for monitoring; Damage may progress with IOP <21 mmHg (normal tension glaucoma).
